notes: OpenCode reset (`/new`) supersession — TARGET spec; NOT yet recorded (driver_gap: slash-command). OpenCode's Source is ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite, core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go): a transient proc-<PID> pre-session is born ready at launch, hands off to a SQLite-keyed UUID-1 session (ses_…) on the first prompt, runs one turn, then a TUI /new (the binary's `session.new` / `New session` command) mints a SECOND ses_… row under the same PID with a fresh session_id and no UUID-1 history; a second prompt drives a turn on that new row. The reset's user-observable signal is v2_session_birth (new_session:true) — a ses_… session_id never seen before appears in ready; the new session superseding the old IS the expected behavior, the same signal the opencode session-resume cell already proves the daemon observes live. As with codex/pi, the old UUID-1 row is retired via the daemon's same-PID cleanup once UUID-2's PID is discovered; there is deliberately NO old_session_ended phase (v2_session_birth is the canonical reset proxy). KNOWN GAP: this cell is by_adapter.opencode.applicable:false — driver_capability:gap:slash-command. drive-opencode-interactive.sh drives BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocesses (case "$TYPE" handles only send|wait_turn|sleep) and (1) headless `opencode run` IGNORES in-TUI slash commands — PROBED 2026-05-26: `opencode run "/new"` stored the literal `/new` as a normal user message in ONE ses_… row, no reset — and (2) the driver has no reset_session step to detect a /new-spawned second ses_… and re-point subsequent turns at it (no session.uuids PLURAL list). The v2_* phases below describe the LIVE-observable arc a live-REPL opencode driver would produce, but are not yet validated against a committed recording. Recording deferred until the opencode interactive driver gains a live-REPL reset_session primitive — a developer task, out of scope for the implement stage.

text: Pre-session row (proc-<PID>) appears in ready within 2s of opencode launch
After the first prompt lands, the SQLite-keyed ses_… session appears in ready — proc-<PID> hands off to a fresh session_id
First user prompt drives ready → working on the ses_… session
First turn completes back to ready (same ses_… session id)

Spec: 'New session appears in ready with a NEW session_id' — after /new, a fresh ses_… session_id never seen before appears in ready; this is the strongest observable proxy for 'the conversation was reset'
Second user prompt drives the NEW session ready → working (must NOT match a stale working transition on UUID-1)
Second turn completes back to ready on the new session
